text: She said the build was ready.
He said they had deployed it that day.
She said she would fix it.
He asked if I could check the logs.
Ana, I missed the incident bridge last night. Can you tell me what the team said?
Of course. Priya said the login service was down for about forty minutes.
David said they had deployed a bad config the night before, and that he would relay the rollback to the team.
And what did the client want?
The client asked whether we could send a written summary that day, so I said we would.
Priya reiterated that the fix was live, but she acknowledged there was still a discrepancy in the logs.
Perfect. Relay all of that to the recipients on the incident thread, and keep their words verbatim.
She said the service was down for about forty minutes.
He said they had deployed a bad config the night before.
The client asked whether we could send a summary that day.
Let me relay their exact words verbatim.
This needs to go up the chain of command.

Let's talk about how to report what people said in a meeting, accurately, without twisting it. When you move from someone's exact words to a report, one thing usually happens: the tense shifts back. If your colleague said 'the build is ready', you report that she said the build was ready. 'Is' becomes 'was'. 'We deployed it today' becomes 'they had deployed it that day'. 'I will fix it' becomes 'he said he would fix it'. Present moves to past, will moves to would, can moves to could. That backshift is not decoration; it tells the listener you are quoting the past, not the present. Now, two warnings. First, do not editorialize. Your job is to relay what was said, not to add your own opinion. If you slip your judgment into the report, people stop trusting you. Second, when the exact wording matters, a number, a commitment, a deadline, do not paraphrase it. Quote it verbatim. Say 'her words were, quote, we ship on Friday, end quote'. For everything else, a clean paraphrase is fine, as long as the meaning survives. Report faithfully, shift the tense, and keep the wording honest. That is how people learn to trust your summaries.

Hi Ana, it's Nadia from the account side, just a quick voice note before you write up the escalation. I was on the call with the client, and I want to make sure the right words go up the chain. The client said the outage had cost them a full morning of orders, and they asked whether we could guarantee it would not happen again. Please notify the incident manager about that, because it needs to go up the chain of command, not stay in our little thread. One thing: when you report the client's commitment, keep it verbatim. Their exact words were, we will accept the fix if you confirm it in writing by Friday. Do not paraphrase that part, because the recipient will act on it. Everything else you can summarize in your own words, just keep the meaning faithful. And please acknowledge my note so I know it reached you. Thanks, Ana, you are really good at this.

Here is how Ana reported the escalation call to her manager. First, she listened carefully and noted the exact wording of what each person had said. Then, she shifted the tenses back so the whole report was in reported speech. After that, she paraphrased the long explanations but kept the key numbers and commitments verbatim. Next, she checked for any discrepancy between what people said and what the logs showed. Finally, she relayed the clean summary to the recipients on the incident thread.
She said the login service was down for about forty minutes.
He said they had deployed a bad config the night before.
The client asked whether we could send a summary that day.
Let me relay their exact words verbatim.
There is a discrepancy between what he said and the logs.
This needs to go up the chain of command.
She said the service was down for about forty minutes.
